startScreenOptions.title: BI12.1-BI12.3 — Interactive Lecture (BI)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for BI12.1-BI12.3.

interactions.label: In paracetamol overdose, the antidote N-acetylcysteine (NAC) protects the liver

params.question: In paracetamol overdose, the antidote N-acetylcysteine (NAC) protects the liver primarily by which mechanism?

answers.text: Inhibiting CYP450 enzymes to prevent NAPQI formation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: NAC does not inhibit CYP450 enzymes. Its mechanism of action is downstream — it provides cysteine so the liver can rebuild its glutathione stores, which then neutralise NAPQI.

answers.text: Directly binding and neutralising NAPQI in the hepatocyte

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: NAC does not neutralise NAPQI directly. It is a cysteine donor that enables glutathione synthesis; the regenerated glutathione is what conjugates and inactivates NAPQI.

answers.text: Replenishing hepatic glutathione, restoring the liver's capacity to neutralise NAPQI

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. NAC is a cysteine precursor that rapidly restores the glutathione depleted by NAPQI, re-establishing the hepatocyte's primary antioxidant defence against oxidative injury.
NAC does not affect renal clearance. Its protective effect is entirely hepatic — restoring glutathione that neutralises the reactive metabolite NAPQI before it damages cell membranes and proteins.

overallFeedback.feedback: NAC works by replenishing hepatic glutathione. In overdose, normal conjugation pathways are overwhelmed and CYP2E1 generates NAPQI faster than glutathione can neutralise it. NAC restores that glutathione pool, halting oxidative hepatocellular necrosis.

Exactly right. NAC provides cysteine for glutathione synthesis. Glutathione then conjugates NAPQI, preventing it from attacking hepatocyte proteins and membranes — which is why early administration is critical.

interactions.label: The body possesses a specific enzyme that directly neutralises the hydroxyl radi

params.question: The body possesses a specific enzyme that directly neutralises the hydroxyl radical.

l10n.wrongAnswerMessage: Actually, the hydroxyl radical has no specific enzymatic defence — unlike superoxide (cleared by SOD) or hydrogen peroxide (cleared by catalase and glutathione peroxidase). It reacts within nanoseconds, making it the most destructive ROS. The body's only strategy is to prevent its formation by clearing upstream precursors.
l10n.correctAnswerMessage: Correct. The hydroxyl radical is uniquely dangerous precisely because no enzyme can directly neutralise it. It reacts within nanoseconds — far faster than any enzymatic defence. This is why upstream enzymes like superoxide dismutase and catalase are critical: they prevent hydroxyl radical formation by clearing its precursors.

interactions.label: In atherosclerosis, oxidised low-density lipoprotein (LDL) contributes to plaque

params.question: In atherosclerosis, oxidised low-density lipoprotein (LDL) contributes to plaque formation primarily because:

answers.text: It directly damages the endothelial lining, creating gaps that cholesterol fills

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: While endothelial dysfunction contributes to atherosclerosis, oxidised LDL's primary pathological role is in foam cell formation — macrophages internalise it through scavenger receptors that lack feedback control, causing uncontrolled cholesterol accumulation.

answers.text: Macrophages internalise it through scavenger receptors that lack feedback inhibition, causing uncontrolled cholesterol accumulation

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct. Unlike normal LDL receptors, scavenger receptors are not downregulated by rising intracellular cholesterol. This leads to massive cholesterol loading, foam cell formation, and the fatty streaks that become atherosclerotic plaques.

answers.text: It inhibits nitric oxide synthesis, causing sustained vasoconstriction and vessel narrowing

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Although oxidised LDL can impair endothelial function, the key mechanism in atherogenesis is macrophage uptake via scavenger receptors without feedback control — leading to foam cells, not vasoconstriction.

answers.text: It activates the complement cascade, triggering inflammatory destruction of the arterial wall

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Inflammation does play a role in atherosclerosis, but the fundamental oxidative-stress mechanism is foam cell formation: macrophages engulf oxidised LDL through scavenger receptors that have no cholesterol-sensing brake.

overallFeedback.feedback: Oxidised LDL is taken up by macrophage scavenger receptors, which crucially lack the feedback inhibition of normal LDL receptors. Without this brake, cholesterol accumulates unchecked, macrophages become foam cells, and atherosclerotic plaques form and progressively narrow the arteries.

Well done. Scavenger receptors on macrophages take up oxidised LDL without the negative feedback that normal LDL receptors have. This uncontrolled uptake produces cholesterol-laden foam cells — the hallmark of early atherosclerotic plaques.
